title: Nuclear Deterrence
description: From RAND's Soviet-era work on game theory to today's current states of concern, such as North Korea and Iran, RAND has applied strategic analysis to international deterrence efforts, with particular focus on the roles of both diplomacy and missile defense systems in global and regional security.
text:
New START’s Expiration Will Make the World Less Safe—Even if It Doesn’t Spark Another Nuclear Arms Race
The expiration of the New START treaty won't trigger an immediate arms race. But without the norms, transparency, and dialogues that have helped manage nuclear competition for decades, the strategic environment will be more dangerous for everyone.
The Israel-Iran Détente Won't Last
Netanyahu's deal with Tehran was more a tactical pause than a game changer in the ongoing rivalry. The hiatus is unlikely to last long, and there are good reasons to believe that the next round of the Iran-Israel war will be even bigger than before.
In Ukraine, Is the U.S. Promising Too Much?
Ukraine will need security guarantees as part of any deal to end the war with Russia. But expansive U.S. commitments may do little to deter future aggression by Moscow—and pose risks for Kyiv, Washington, and U.S. allies in Europe.
The RAND Ontological Model for Assessing Nuclear Crisis Escalation Risk (ROMANCER): Theoretical Introduction and Background
RAND researchers have conceived and prototyped the RAND Ontological Model for Assessing Nuclear Crisis Escalation Risk (ROMANCER). This report describes the motivation and theoretical underpinnings behind ROMANCER and provides some notional outputs.
The RAND Ontological Model for Assessing Nuclear Crisis Escalation Risk (ROMANCER): User Guide for Version 1.0
This user guide serves as a companion to the RAND Ontological Model for Assessing Nuclear Crisis Escalation Risk (ROMANCER) codebase. It provides a comprehensive understanding of ROMANCER, from initial setup to advanced use and development.
Putin's Failed Nuclear Threats
Brandishing nuclear arms and threats of doubtful military value may be signs of Kremlin battlefield frustration or political desperation. Irresponsible and irrational nuclear threats will not offer Putin relief from his narrowing options.
Can Europe Deter Russia?
In this RAND Europe webinar, experts discuss the lengths to which Europe must go to defend itself and to deter Russia.
Pakistan and Saudi Arabia Mutual Defense Pact: Important to Whom?
What is the significance of the Pakistan–Saudi Arabia defense pact? In its early days, the agreement's impact on India has received little attention.
Can Europe Deter Russia?
In this RAND Europe Webinar, experts will discuss the lengths Europe must go to defend itself and to deter Russia. They will reflect on recent agreements between the likes of Germany, France and the UK, and the prospects for deepening cooperation on defence industrial capacity as well as military interoperability. The discussion will also draw on recent RAND Europe research which sheds light on Russia’s perception of the European deterrents.
